Health

Home
Health

Health

Gain insights into the role your genes play in your health

Get in touch

The future of health through genetic insights

Our health products provide you with personalized insights into genetic traits that—according to scientific studies—may influence how your body responds to certain nutrients, lifestyle factors, or environmental stimuli.

Harness the potential of genetic information to better understand your individual traits and support your well-being in a targeted way.*

Sensors for cardiovascular risk

Cardiovascular Health Sensor

Understanding the genetic factors behind your heart health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Cardiovascular Health Sensor

The Cardiovascular Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of over 18 relevant gene variations

Assessment of the individual risk for cardiovascular diseases

Review of the efficacy of 117 relevant medicinal products

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

A detailed evaluation of the test results

Concrete recommendations for prevention

Cardiovascular diseases are widespread

Problems with the cardiovascular system are widespread and lead to complaints in more and more people. Depending on the type of disease, they can lead to permanent impairment and, in the worst case, can even be life-threatening.

A number of risk factors can lead to cardiovascular diseases. These include obesity, diabetes, high blood pressure, excessive alcohol consumption, and smoking.

The genetic predisposition for cardiovascular diseases

In addition to the lifestyle factors mentioned above, genetic predisposition also contributes to cardiovascular diseases.

Certain genes can significantly raise the risk of cardiovascular issues. Therefore, analyzing these relevant genes can be valuable in identifying potential risks early on, helping individuals take preventive measures when necessary.

How our Cardiovascular Sensor works

Our Cardiovascular Sensor provides a comprehensive analysis, testing 18+ genetic variants linked to cardiovascular disease and assessing genetic influences on 117 drugs.

The results are detailed in a report with tailored prevention recommendations. This analysis supports early detection and helps reduce risks by guiding effective lifestyle adjustments.

What formats and file sizes can I upload?

You can add the following formats with a maximum size of 7MB: DOC, PDF, JPG, TNP, GIF. If you have larger files (e.g., graphic samples), you can also provide us with a Dropbox link or similar.

If there is no suitable position available, can I still apply speculatively?

Yes, absolutely! Simply click the Speculative Application button and upload your resume. Who knows, you might be the perfect fit for our team.

Can I apply for mulitple positions?

Please send us your resume only once. If you wish to apply for multiple current job openings, please inform us in your cover letter. We will carefully consider your request.

Hypertension Sensor

How your genes are linked to blood pressure

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Hypertension Sensor

The Hypertension Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of 4 relevant genetic variations

Determination of the individual risk for high blood pressure

Analysis of the effects of more than 65 relevant drugs

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

Written report with all results

Individual recommendations for prevention

High blood pressure is often detected too late

High blood pressure (hypertension) is not painful and often does not cause any symptoms for a long time. Accordingly, it is usually detected much too late and may already have caused severe damage to the heart and blood vessels.

Early diagnosis of high blood pressure is crucial to avoid such problems. After all, hypertension is one of the most significant risk factors for heart attacks, strokes and other dangerous diseases of the cardiovascular system.

Causes of hypertension

In 90 percent of all cases, hypertension is usually not linked to organic disease but often arises without a clear cause. Contributing factors include obesity, inactivity, smoking, and high salt intake, while genetic predisposition also plays a crucial role.

Genetic testing can help identify individual risks early, offering a proactive approach to managing hypertension effectively.

How our Hypertension Sensor works

Our Hypertension Sensor analyzes four genetic variants that may increase high blood pressure risk. Results, including assessments of over 65 drugs, are provided in a detailed report with personalized prevention recommendations.

This analysis helps detect hypertension early, allowing patients to take necessary countermeasures to manage their risk effectively.

Thrombo Sensor

Insight into genetically influenced thrombosis factors

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Thrombo Sensor

The Thrombo Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the three gene variations relevant to the risk of thrombosis

Assessment of the individual risk of arterial and venous thrombosis

Assessment of potentially unsuitable medicinal products based on the gene profile

Reliable examination by our laboratory

Detailed written evaluation of the results

Individual recommendations for prevention

Thromboses pose a significant threat

Thrombosis is a disease that leads to the formation of blood clots. These can clog vessels and block the blood supply to the affected areas. This can cause permanent damage and, in the worst case, can even be life-threatening.

The impact varies by location: a clot blocking blood to the brain causes a stroke, while one obstructing blood flow to the heart triggers a heart attack.

Genetic defects can increase the risk of thrombosis

In principle, anyone can develop thrombosis, although the risk is particularly high in women during pregnancy and childbirth.

Irrespective of this, certain genetic defects can further increase the risk of developing thrombosis. Given the considerable risks that thrombosis can pose, it is, therefore, advisable to have an analysis of the relevant genes carried out.

How our Thrombo Sensor works

Our Thrombo Sensor analyzes genetic variations linked to thrombosis risk and assesses whether specific drugs may pose problems due to a patient’s genetic profile.

We determine the risk for venous or arterial thrombosis and recommend preventive measures. A detailed report presents results and tailored recommendations, aiding in the early prevention of thrombosis and management of risks.

Sensors for digestive health

Lactose Sensor

Understanding your genetic ability to process lactose

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Lactose Sensor

The Lactose Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the relevant genetic variation

Assessment of the individual risk for lactose intolerance

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

A clear summary of the results

Individual nutritional recommendations based on the results

Many people suffer from lactose intolerance

Milk is considered healthy and is particularly recommended as a source of calcium for healthy bones. In principle, there is nothing wrong with this. However, many people suffer from lactose intolerance and, therefore, cannot tolerate dairy products.

It is estimated that every fourth to sixth person in Europe is affected by this particular form of food intolerance.

Causes of lactose intolerance

Lactose in milk is broken down by the enzyme lactase in the intestine before entering the bloodstream. Newborns naturally produce lactase to digest breast milk, but production decreases over time.

For some individuals, a genetic defect may lead to complete lactose intolerance, causing severe digestive issues after consuming lactose-containing foods.

How our Lactose Sensor works

Our Lactose Sensor analyzes samples to determine if genetic variations lead to lactose intolerance and ensure adequate calcium intake. This assessment allows us to evaluate individual risk and recommend dietary changes if necessary.

We provide a clear written report summarizing the results, aiding in the early detection of lactose intolerance and promoting effective dietary adjustments.

IBD Sensor

Gaining insight into genetic factors related to gut health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

IBD Sensor

The IBD Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of 3 gene variations relevant to Crohn’s disease

Assessment of the risk of contracting Crohn’s disease

Reliable examination in our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Individual recommendations for action

Crohn’s disease can cause massive discomfort

Crohn’s disease is an inflammatory bowel disease that causes chronic and intermittent symptoms, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and fatigue. It can lead to nutrient deficiencies and inflammation in other areas, such as the joints.

The disease’s progression varies among patients, and ulcers may develop in the intestine over time in some cases.

Causes of Crohn’s disease

The exact causes of Crohn’s disease have not yet clear. In addition to an unhealthy lifestyle, genetic changes play a particularly important role in Crohn’s disease.

About half of all those affected have specific genetic defects that influence the immune system. For this reason, genetic analysis can help to assess the individual risk of Crohn’s disease and take appropriate preventive measures if necessary.

How our IBD Sensor works

Our IBD Sensor analyzes submitted samples for three genetic variations in the NOD2 gene linked to Crohn’s disease. We also assess the effects of 21 relevant drugs based on genetic profiles to evaluate Crohn’s disease risk and necessary preventive measures.

After analysis, we provide a detailed report with results and recommendations, aiding early detection and dietary symptom management.

Gluten Sensor

How your genes are linked to gluten response

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Gluten Sensor

The Gluten Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the two relevant genetic variations

Assessment of the individual risk for gluten intolerance

Reliable test execution in our laboratory

Written report with the results of the investigation

Concrete nutritional recommendations based on the test results

Gluten is causing problems for more and more people

Gluten is the name given to a mixture of certain proteins contained in some cereals. The so-called gluten intolerance (celiac disease) is a widespread food intolerance that affects approximately one percent of the population in western countries.

If there is hypersensitivity to the components of gluten, this is particularly noticeable in the form of digestive problems such as diarrhea, flatulence, and stomach pain.

Genetic defects increase the risk of gluten intolerance

Two specific genetic defects often cause gluten intolerance, prompting the immune system to treat gluten as a threat. This response triggers chronic inflammation, leading to various health issues and increasing bowel cancer risk if dietary adjustments aren’t made.

For suspected gluten intolerance, genetic analysis can provide clarity and guidance on necessary dietary changes.

How our Gluten Sensor works

Our Gluten Sensor analyzes samples for two genetic variations linked to gluten intolerance. Based on the results, we assess individual risk levels and recommend dietary adjustments if necessary.

After completing the analysis, we provide a written evaluation detailing results and recommendations, enabling effective early detection and prevention to avoid health issues related to celiac disease.

Holistic health sensors

Breast Health Sensor

Understanding genetic factors related to breast health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Breast Health Sensor

The Breast Health Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of the ten genes relevant for breast cancer risk

Assessment of the individual risk

Analysis of the efficacy of 66 relevant drugs

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

A written summary of the results

Concrete recommendations for effective prevention

Breast cancer is on the rise

Breast cancer cases have nearly doubled in the past 50 years, making it the most common cancer among women in Western countries. Despite decreasing mortality rates due to medical advancements, more women still die from breast cancer than any other cancer.

Thus, awareness of risk factors and prevention is essential, as early diagnosis of hypertension is crucial to prevent heart attacks and strokes.

Causes of breast cancer

Breast cancer often results from a combination of environmental factors and genetic defects, leading some women to face a higher risk due to genetic predisposition and unhealthy lifestyles.

Currently, ten genes are linked to breast cancer development. Genetic testing can assess these risks, enabling the implementation of necessary preventive measures if needed.

How our Breast Health Sensor works

Our Breast Health Sensor analyzes ten genes in the sample that influence breast cancer risk and tests 66 medications for potential genetic issues. This assessment helps determine a patient’s likelihood of developing breast cancer and the need for preventive measures.

After the examination, we provide a written evaluation presenting all relevant results to guide individual risk management and prevention strategies.

Prostate Health Sensor

Insight into genetic factors affecting prostate health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Prostate Health Sensor

The Prostate Health Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of 10 genetic variations relevant to prostate cancer

Individual assessment of the risk of developing prostate cancer

Investigation of the effects of the genetic profile on 43 drugs

A clear summary of the test results

Concrete recommendations for action based on the results

Prostate cancer is widespread in men

Prostate cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors diagnosed in men and ranks third in terms of death rates.

The risk increases significantly with age, which is why regular prostate examinations are urgently recommended from the age of 45 at the latest. After all, in many cases, it is possible to successfully treat prostate cancer with an early diagnosis.

Individual risk factors for prostate cancer

Age is the primary risk factor for prostate cancer, but genetic predisposition also significantly influences whether a patient develops the disease and its progression. Therefore, genetic testing can be beneficial.

Analyzing relevant genes helps reliably assess individual risk, allowing for appropriate measures to be implemented if necessary.

How the Prostate Health Sensor works

Our Prostate Health Sensor analyzes samples for ten genetic variants linked to prostate cancer development. We also evaluate the patient’s genetic profile for potential efficacy and tolerability issues with 43 different drugs.

After completing the analysis, we provide a detailed written evaluation with results and actionable recommendations, aiding in early risk identification and timely intervention for prostate cancer.

Lung Health Sensor

Understanding genetic influences on lung function

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Lung Health Sensor

The Lung Health Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of 5 gene variations relevant for lung cancer risk

Assessment of the risk of developing lung cancer

Evaluation of the effect of 40 drugs based on the gene profile

Reliable analysis by our laboratory

Written report with all results

Concrete recommendations for prevention

The mortality rate for lung cancer is very high

Lung cancer is one of the most common cancers, with a high mortality rate, being the second leading cause of cancer death in women and the most common in men.

Late diagnosis often leads to advanced disease.

Smoking is the primary risk factor, responsible for approximately 85 percent of all lung cancer cases.

The influence of genes on the risk of lung cancer

The lungs are regularly exposed to harmful environmental influences, such as exhaust fumes from cars. Under normal circumstances, the immune system recognizes and neutralizes them in good time.

However, certain genetic variations in many people lead to this detoxification function of the body not functioning correctly, and thus, the risk of lung cancer increases considerably over time.

How our Lung Health Sensor works

Our Lung Health Sensor analyzes five genetic variations affecting lung cancer risk, enabling an assessment of individual susceptibility. We also evaluate 40 different drugs to determine any limitations in efficacy and tolerability based on the patient’s genetic profile.

After completing the analysis, we provide a written evaluation with relevant results and recommendations for effective early detection and risk reduction.

Skin Health Sensor

Gaining insight into genetic traits related to skin

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Skin Health Sensor

The Skin Health Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of 18 relevant genetic variations

Assessment of the genetic skin cancer risk

Evaluation of the efficacy of 27 drugs based on their genetic profile

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

A Written evaluation of the results

Individual recommendations for prevention

Early detection of skin cancer is particularly important

Usually, skin cancer is comparatively easy to diagnose and, if diagnosed early, can be treated well. However, the tumors often develop inconspicuously at first and only cause discomfort once the skin cancer has spread.

Regular preventive examinations are therefore useful and can help to detect not only white but also particularly dangerous black skin cancer in good time and to take the necessary treatment measures.

Causes of skin cancer

Skin cancer is primarily caused by excessive UV radiation, putting outdoor workers, especially in construction, at higher risk, where it’s recognized as an occupational disease. However, genetic factors also significantly influence individual risk.

Analyzing relevant genetic variations, as conducted with our Skin Health Sensor, is often beneficial for assessing this risk.

How our Skin Health Sensor works

Our Skin Health Sensor analyzes samples for 18 genetic variations linked to white or black skin cancer, enabling us to assess the patient’s genetic risk. We also evaluate the efficacy of 27 drugs based on their genetic profile.

Following the analysis, we provide a written evaluation with results and personalized prevention recommendations to help reduce skin cancer risk and enable early detection.

Colon Health Sensor

Understanding genetic traits related to colon health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Colon Health Sensor

The Colon Health Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of 17 relevant gene variations

Assessment of the genetic risk of colorectal cancer

Assessment of the effect of 32 drugs based on their genetic profile

Reliable testing in our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Individual precautionary recommendations based on the results

Bowel cancer is one of the most common types of cancer

Colorectal cancer is one of the most common cancers in Western industrial nations, affecting about six percent of the population, with men at slightly higher risk than women.

Despite advancements in treatment options, approximately 27,000 people die from colorectal cancer each year in Germany alone, highlighting the critical importance of early diagnosis and treatment for a favorable prognosis.

Causes of colorectal cancer

The risk of developing bowel cancer sharply increases with age, and unhealthy habits, such as excessive consumption of sausage and red meat, significantly contribute to this risk.

Additionally, genetic factors play a crucial role in colorectal cancer development. Genetic analysis can help assess individual risk factors, enabling timely preventive measures if necessary.

How the Colon Health Sensor works

Our Colon Health Sensor conducts a comprehensive analysis of samples, examining 17 gene variations linked to colorectal cancer risk. This assessment helps determine a patient’s likelihood of developing the disease and whether special precautions are needed.

Following the analysis, we provide a detailed evaluation of individual risk and the effects of 32 relevant drugs, aiding colorectal cancer screening and risk reduction.

Metabolic health sensors

Iron Sensor

Analyzing genetic indicators of iron metabolism

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Iron Sensor

The Iron Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the three genetic variations relevant for iron uptake

Review of the risk of contracting hemochromatosis

Reliable examination by our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Written report with all results

Individual recommendations for iron uptake based on the results

Iron is indispensable for the oxygen supply

Iron is a trace element that plays a vital role in the human body. In addition to its participation in various metabolic processes, it is an essential requirement for the formation of hemoglobin.

Thus, iron is directly involved in the storage and transport of oxygen. A sufficient uptake of this trace element is therefore indispensable for a functioning organism.

An oversupply of iron is dangerous

While essential, excess iron can be dangerous, causing long-term health issues and potential organ damage. Individuals with hemochromatosis—a hereditary condition causing increased iron absorption in the small intestine—are particularly at risk.

A genetic test can help diagnose this iron storage disease early, allowing for timely intervention and management.

How our Iron Sensor works

Our Iron Sensor analyzes blood samples for three genetic variations affecting iron absorption. This determines if the body is absorbing excess iron due to a hereditary iron storage disorder.

After the analysis, we provide a detailed report assessing iron storage disease risk and offer personalized recommendations. Our Iron Sensor supports health prevention and helps prevent iron-related organ damage.

What formats and file sizes can I upload?

You can add the following formats with a maximum size of 7MB: DOC, PDF, JPG, TNP, GIF. If you have larger files (e.g., graphic samples), you can also provide us with a Dropbox link or similar.

Diabetes Sensor

Discovering genetic influences on glucose metabolism

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Diabetes Sensor

The Diabetes Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the nine genes relevant to diabetes

Verification of the tolerability of 36 important drugs

Reliable examination by our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Individual recommendations for prevention

Diabetes is becoming a widespread disease

In recent decades, diabetes has developed into a widespread disease. In Germany alone, around six million people suffer from this metabolic illness and the consequences of impaired regulation of blood sugar levels.

The by far most significant number is affected by type 2 diabetes. If left untreated, the disease can cause further serious health problems, including severe organ damage and blindness.

Genetic predisposition to type 2 diabetes

The risk of developing type 2 diabetes varies from person to person. Factors such as incorrect eating habits and obesity play an important role. However, it is primarily the genetic predisposition that decides whether a person develops type 2 diabetes in the course of his or her life.

In order to assess the actual risk in individual cases, genetic analysis can help by examining the genes relevant in this context.

How our Diabetes Sensor works

Our Diabetes Sensor performs an in-depth genetic analysis, examining nine variations linked to type 2 diabetes risk. This information aids in prevention and potential future treatment.
After analysis, we provide a detailed report with individual risk assessment and tailored nutritional recommendations. Our Diabetes Sensor supports preventive healthcare, helping at-risk patients make meaningful lifestyle adjustments.

Premium Plus Sensor

Comprehensive genetic analysis for personalized recommendations

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Premium Plus Sensor

The Premium Plus Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of more than 140 health-relevant genetic variations

Assessment of the individual risk for more than 35 diseases

Reliable analysis in our laboratory

Detailed written evaluation of the test results

Tailor-made recommendations of the test results

Health problems often develop slowly

There is hardly a person who does not get one or the other problem with his health in the course of his life. In most cases, the symptoms develop slowly and are therefore often taken seriously too late.

Early prevention can often make a significant contribution to preventing diseases in the first place or at least treating them successfully. For this reason, early diagnosis is essential.

The influence of genes on health

Genes strongly influence many potential health risks. Genetic defects can increase the likelihood of the development of many diseases and lead
to massive health problems in both men and women.

Therefore, it is useful to carry out genetic analysis to detect possible diseases at an early stage. Such an analysis can reliably identify potential risks so that suitable preventive measures can be taken in good time.

How our Premium Plus Sensor works

The Premium Plus Sensor offers our most thorough genetic analysis, testing over 110 disease-related genetic variations and assessing the impact on approximately 230 drugs. This enables us to identify genetic risks for more than 35 diseases and recommend preventive measures.

Following analysis, we provide a detailed report, making our Premium Plus Sensor invaluable for comprehensive health care.

Health sensors for the musculoskeletal system

Bone Health Sensor

Analyzing genetic traits related to bone health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Bone Health Sensor

The Bone Health Sensor at a glance:

Examination of 4 genes relevant to bone health

Assessment of the risk of developing osteoporosis

Evaluation of the effect of 26 drugs based on their genetic profile

Reliable analysis method in our laboratory

Written report with all test results

Individual recommendations for necessary preventive measures

Osteoporosis is widespread

Osteoporosis, a common bone disease affecting mostly the elderly, leads to progressive bone mass loss, often called bone atrophy. As bone density decreases, bones become more porous and fracture easily.

In advanced cases, even lifting a slightly heavier bag can cause fractures, as bone resorption significantly weakens bone strength over time.

Causes of osteoporosis

Women have a significantly higher risk of osteoporosis, with around 80% of cases occurring post-menopause. Smoking also increases this risk. Various genes impact bone density, influencing osteoporosis development.

Since the disease often goes undetected until late, genetic analysis is a useful tool for early detection and management of bone loss.

How our Bone Health Sensor works

Our Bone Health Sensor analyzes four osteoporosis-related genes and assesses how 26 drugs interact with the genetic profile. This helps determine individual osteoporosis risk and enables personalized prevention recommendations.

After analysis, we provide a detailed report assessing bone loss risk. The Bone Health Sensor supports early detection, helping patients address osteoporosis proactively.

Joint Sensor

Understanding genetic influences on joint function

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Joint Sensor

The Joint Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the two relevant genes

Review of genetic predisposition to joint inflammation

Assessment of the influence of genes on the intake of 56 drugs

Reliable test execution by our laboratory

Written report with all results

Individual recommendations for prevention

Inflamed joints often have severe consequences

Numerous inflammatory joint diseases, which may occur even in youth, can severely impact quality of life. In severe cases, these diseases lead to progressive mobility impairment or physical disability.

As healing is generally not possible, early diagnosis of inflammatory joint diseases is crucial for effective management and prevention of further complications.

Genetic predisposition to inflammatory joint diseases

A malfunction of the immune system causes inflammations in the joints. The immune system erroneously classifies parts of the joint as a threat and tries to combat them.

The patient’s genetic predisposition largely determines the probability of inflammatory joint diseases. An analysis of the relevant genes can, therefore, help to identify risks at an early stage and counteract them in good time.

How our Joint Sensor works

Our Joint Sensor analyzes genetic variants linked to joint inflammation and evaluates how 56 drugs interact with the patient’s genetic profile. This helps assess the individual risk of inflammatory joint diseases and allows for personalized prevention recommendations.

After analysis, we provide a detailed report with test results and actionable advice, supporting early diagnosis and effective prevention.

Health sensors for the eyes

AMD Sensor

Genetic indicators of age-related vision changes

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

AMD Sensor

The AMD Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of three relevant genes

Assessment of the risk of developing age-related macular degeneration

Reliable examination in our laboratory

Written report with all important results

Individual recommendations for effective prevention

Age-dependent macular degeneration is often detected late

Eyesight typically worsens with age, but for some, vision loss goes beyond normal age-related changes. Diseases like age-related macular degeneration (AMD) can cause progressive vision loss, especially in the area of sharpest sight.

AMD comes in various forms and leads to significant visual impairment beyond standard age-related decline.

Possible causes of AMD

The exact causes of age-related macular degeneration are still unclear. However, we know that at least three different genes have a significant influence on the probability of developing AMD.

Therefore, genetic analysis can help to determine the individual risk for age-related macular degeneration and to detect symptoms at an early stage.

How our AMD Sensor works

Through our AMD Sensor, we conduct a genetic analysis of the sample submitted to assess three genes associated with an increased risk of age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

This helps determine individual susceptibility and guide preventive measures. After analysis, we provide a detailed report with test results and specific recommendations, aiding in early detection and support for vision health.

Glaucoma Sensor

Genetic factors related to intraocular pressure

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Glaucoma Sensor

The Glaucoma Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the gene relevant for glaucoma

Evaluation of the individual glaucoma risk

Evaluation of the effect of 17 drugs based on the gene profile

Reliable testing by our laboratory

Written report with all results

Concrete recommendations for action based on the test results

Glaucoma causes irreversible damage

Glaucoma is widespread. It is estimated that about 500,000 people suffer from glaucoma in Germany alone. Glaucoma causes irreversible damage to the nerve fibers of affected people and even leads to a complete loss of vision in about 10 percent of all people affected.

Timely treatment can reliably prevent permanent damage in most cases. Early diagnosis is therefore essential, which is why the necessary examinations should not be left out.

The influence of genes on the risk of glaucoma

Glaucoma develops slowly and is not noticed by those affected for a long time. By the time vision is noticeably impaired, the majority of existing photoreceptors are permanently destroyed.

It is now known that a particular gene has a decisive influence on the probability of developing glaucoma. A genetic test can, therefore, help to identify individual risks and, if necessary, arrange for appropriate eye examinations.

This is how the Glaucoma Sensor works

Our Glaucoma Sensor analyzes the submitted sample, focusing on the LOXL1 gene linked to glaucoma. We also assess how the genetic profile affects the efficacy and tolerability of 17 relevant drugs.

This enables us to evaluate the patient’s genetic risk for glaucoma and recommend eye examinations. A detailed report follows, aiding in early detection and prevention of eye damage.

Sensors for cognitive and mental health

ADHD Sensor

Genetic indicators related to attention function

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

ADHD Sensor

The ADHD Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of the five relevant genes

Assessment of the genetic risk for ADHD

Evaluation of the efficacy of 16 drugs based on the gene profile

Reliable analysis in our laboratory

Detailed report with all results

ADHD has different faces

ADHD is common, affecting nearly one in ten school-age children, with boys diagnosed about twice as often as girls. The disorder presents in various forms: some patients are primarily inattentive, others predominantly hyperactive, or exhibit both symptoms.

Depending on its severity, ADHD can significantly impact the daily lives of those affected and those around them.

The influence of genes on the development of ADHD

Various factors favor the development of ADHD. These include several environmental factors, such as drug abuse during pregnancy or a disturbed parent-child relationship.

However, genes also play an essential role in the development of ADHD. In about 75 percent of all cases, certain genetic variants can be detected in people with ADHD. In the case of already existing behavioral disorders, a genetic test is, therefore, often useful to make a diagnosis.

How the ADHD Sensor works

For our ADHD Sensor, we conduct a genetic analysis to examine five genes linked to ADHD. We also evaluate how the genetic profile affects the efficacy of 16 relevant medications. This enables us to assess genetic risk and recommend appropriate treatments.

After analysis, we provide a clear, detailed report, aiding in diagnosing behavioral disorders and determining effective treatment measures.

Depression Sensor

Impact of genetic factors on mood and stress response

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Depression Sensor

The Depression Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of 8 relevant genetic variations

Determination of the individual risk for depression

Investigation of the tolerability of 59 common drugs

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Personalized recommendations for action based on the results

Depression is widespread

Depression is a prevalent mental illness, affecting about seven percent of adults. Those impacted often struggle with eating, sleeping, and social interactions, leading to a significant decline in quality of life.

Early diagnosis is vital, as untreated depression can persist for years and severely impact well-being. Awareness and intervention are crucial for effective management.

Causes of depression

Various factors contribute to the development of depression, such as traumatic events or pregnancy. Additionally, certain genes significantly influence an individual’s risk.

A genetic test that analyzes relevant gene variations can be valuable in identifying this risk early, allowing for timely intervention and potentially mitigating the onset of psychological problems associated with depression.

How our Depression Sensor works

For our Depression Sensor, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the submitted sample, examining eight genetic variations linked to depression. This allows us to assess a patient’s likelihood of developing depression and the potential effectiveness of antidepressant treatment.

After analysis, we provide a detailed report outlining individual risks and appropriate preventive measures, aiding early recognition and effective intervention.

Schizophrenia Sensor

Genetic traits linked to mental resilience

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Schizophrenia Sensor

The Schizophrenia Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the four relevant gene variants

Assessment of the risk of developing schizophrenia

Reliable examination by our laboratory

Written report with all results

Individual evaluation of the effects of appropriate drugs

Schizophrenia is a significant burden

Schizophrenia is a severe, chronic mental illness characterized by symptoms such as delusions, hallucinations, concentration problems, and diminished pleasure.

Depending on its severity, those affected may lose touch with reality, resulting in a significant burden for both the patient and their loved ones. This disorder profoundly impacts daily life and relationships.

Genetic causes of schizophrenia

The exact causes of schizophrenia are not yet clear. However, it is believed that the disease is triggered by an interplay of psychosocial and biological factors.

Genetic predisposition plays a vital role in this. The genes have a significant influence on the individual’s risk of developing schizophrenia.

How our Schizophrenia Sensor works

For our Schizophrenia Sensor, we conduct a thorough analysis of the submitted sample, examining four genetic variations linked to schizophrenia. This allows us to assess the individual’s risk of developing the disorder and recommend suitable treatment options.

After analysis, we provide a detailed written evaluation, explaining the likelihood of various schizophrenia symptoms, aiding early detection and timely treatment.

Alzheimer Sensor

Genetic influences on neural function

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Alzheimer Sensor

The Alzheimer Sensor at a glance:

Analysis of the two gene variants relevant to the risk of Alzheimer’s disease

Verification of the tolerability of 75 important drugs

Reliable test execution in our laboratory

Written evaluation of the test results

Individual recommendations for effective prevention

When memory slowly fades

Alzheimer’s disease typically affects individuals over 65, leading to the progressive loss of cognitive abilities due to the death of certain brain regions. Initial symptoms, like short-term memory issues and difficulty understanding speech, can appear up to eight years before a definitive diagnosis.

Among 24 million dementia patients globally, approximately 60 percent have Alzheimer’s disease.

Possible causes of AMD

So far, it is not exactly clear what causes Alzheimer’s disease. However, we know that certain genetic defects can significantly increase the risk of Alzheimer’s disease.

Therefore, an analysis of the relevant genes can help to determine the probability of contracting Alzheimer’s disease. The insights gained can then be used, among other things, to take appropriate precautionary measures at an early stage.

How our AMD Sensor works

For our Alzheimer Sensor, we analyze the submitted sample, focusing on two gene variants linked to Alzheimer’s risk. The results help determine a patient’s likelihood of developing the disease and guide dietary recommendations regarding antioxidants.

After completing the analysis, we provide a concise report with actionable prevention strategies, aiding early risk identification and lifestyle adjustments.

Additional health sensors

Periodontitis Sensor

Genetic indicators related to periodontal health

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Periodontitis Sensor

The Periodontitis Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of the five genes relevant for periodontitis

Determination of the individual risk for periodontosis

Reliable testing by our laboratory

Written report with the results of the investigation

Recommendations for effective prevention based on the results

Periodontal disease is widespread

Periodontal disease is one of the most common oral diseases, affecting over 50 percent of adults aged 35 to 44. Many suffer from severe gum disease, known as periodontitis, caused by bacteria that inflame the gums.

If untreated, these bacteria can weaken the immune system, leading to attacks on the jawbone and potential tooth loss.

Causes of periodontitis

Periodontal disease arises from several factors, with oral hygiene being crucial. Inadequate oral care significantly increases the risk of gum inflammation and its consequences.

Additionally, specific genetic defects can cause an overreaction of the immune system, leading to severe periodontal disease. A genetic test can provide clarity and help assess individual risks effectively.

How our Periodontitis Sensor works

As part of our Periodontitis Sensor, our laboratory analyzes five genes related to periodontitis and four genetic variants affecting titanium dental implant success. This enables early preventive measures and appropriate treatment selection for existing periodontal disease.

The results are summarized in a structured report with actionable recommendations, helping to support dental health and patient prophylaxis effectively.

Pharmaco Sensor

Genetic influences on drug metabolism

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

Pharmaco Sensor

The Pharmaco Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of more than 70 relevant gene variants

Individual evaluation of more than 2,000 drugs

Reliable test procedure in our laboratory

Clear report with all results

Drugs do not all work for all in the same way

Ideally, a drug helps to treat disease and cure the patient. However, as enormous as medical progress has been in recent decades, two people may react differently to taking the same drug.

While one patient benefits considerably from the medication, another may suffer from severe side effects, some of which can even be life-threatening.

Causes of differences in the effects of a drug

The reason why drugs are tolerated differently by different people lies in the genes. Some genetic defects can lead to a drug not working properly or not being tolerated.

This risk should not be underestimated, given that adverse drug reactions are the fifth most common cause of death in Western countries. An analysis of the relevant genes in this context helps to avoid potential problems in the first place and to ensure optimal treatment success.

How our Pharmaco Sensor works

As part of our Pharmaco Sensor, we analyze over 70 genetic variations related to drug efficacy and tolerability. This allows us to evaluate the effectiveness and potential intolerances of more than 2,000 common medications based on a patient’s genetic profile.

Our comprehensive report provides results and recommendations to help assess side effect risks and identify unsuitable drugs.

HIV Resistance Sensor

Genetic traits related to immune response

Learn more
Mehr erfahren

HIV Resistance Sensor

The HIV Resistance Sensor at a glance:

Investigation of the CCR5 gene relevant for HIV infection

Assessment of the individual risk of infection

Analysis of the efficacy of more than 25 relevant drugs

Reliable testing by our laboratory

A written summary of the results

HIV is a global problem

The Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) has spread globally since the 1980s, leading to nearly 40 million deaths. Despite medical advancements and declining death rates, over 1.2 million people died from HIV-related causes in 2014.

HIV is transmitted through contact with body fluids, with unprotected sexual intercourse being the most common route of infection.

The impact of genes on the risk of infection

While there is no absolute protection against HIV infection, individuals are not equally at risk upon exposure. Genetic predisposition can lead to significant differences that either increase or decrease the likelihood of contracting the virus.

An analysis of the relevant genes can help determine which risk group a person belongs to.

How our HIV Resistance Sensor works

For our HIV Resistance Sensor, we analyze the CCR5 gene linked to HIV infection risk and around 40 gene variants affecting the efficacy of 30 drugs. This test helps determine whether a patient is at high or low risk of HIV infection and suggests appropriate treatments.

After completion, we provide a precise written evaluation of the results.

What formats and file sizes can I upload?

You can add the following formats with a maximum size of 7MB: DOC, PDF, JPG, TNP, GIF. If you have larger files (e.g., graphic samples), you can also provide us with a Dropbox link or similar.

If there is no suitable position available, can I still apply speculatively?

Yes, absolutely! Simply click the Speculative Application button and upload your resume. Who knows, you might be the perfect fit for our team.

Can I apply for mulitple positions?

Please send us your resume only once. If you wish to apply for multiple current job openings, please inform us in your cover letter. We will carefully consider your request.

DNA PLUS genetic analysis: Key features and scientific foundation

This analysis is not offered by Novogenia. It is created and sold by our partner company DNA PLUS - Zentrum für Humangenetik GmbH in Germany.

  • The genetic traits included in this analysis are supported by scientific studies.
  • The recommendations derived from them are based on logical conclusions.*
  • Customers report high levels of satisfaction.
  • Internal evaluations assess how recommendations are implemented.
  • External studies examine aspects of the underlying recommendations.*

Disclaimer

Science: Today, there are already about 4 million scientific publications that have studied the effects of certain gene defects on the human body. A typical finding from this sounds something like this: “If you have gene defect X, vitamin Y cannot be converted and remains ineffective.” Only when the effects of a gene defect have been independently demonstrated by at least three different studies is the gene test included in the program. Therefore, the influence of a gene defect is always backed by several scientific studies, and you can find the sources for this in the back part of each chapter. The genetic traits given by this analysis are thus considered to be based on scientific studies. 

Recommendation: Recommendations based on your genetic profile. The recommendations derived from your genetic traits are often not determined by studies, but are often logical conclusions. An example: If a certain vitamin doesn’t work due to a gene variation, the conclusion is to switch to another vitamin with a similar effect. We achieve this for example by changing your diet or supplementation. So, it is important to understand that the recommendations developed by our experts are not based on randomized, placebo-controlled studies, but were created as logical conclusions based on your genetic traits. 

Back to Top

Important Information

We sell our products exclusively through partner companies. We are happy to receive your inquiry and establish contact with a suitable partner company in your area.

Scientific Information

That genes affect body weight, the effectiveness of certain weight loss strategies, and the ability to deal with certain nutrients is supported by multiple scientific studies for each gene. Therefore, the genetic traits determined by this analysis are considered scientifically confirmed.

The adaptations of micronutrient dosing or dietary recommendations derived from these scientific findings require the so-called gold standard for clear scientific proof – these are randomised, placebo-controlled studies. These already exist for folic acid and vitamin B2 but have not yet been published for all genetic effects in this programme. For this reason, the genetic adaptation of micronutrient dosing and food evaluation should be considered logical conclusions and not scientifically proven. Your optional supplements are thus scientifically tailored to your lifestyle, your blood values, and additionally experimentally to your genes – depending on your choice
of analyses.
